content.text: Team Health Scorecard Metrics for Claude Teams

A team health scorecard is a weekly, quantitative view of how well a Claude integration team is maintaining the engineering standards that keep their AI features reliable, safe, and cost-controlled. Unlike per-release checklists, which verify a single release, the scorecard tracks trends: whether the team is improving, holding steady, or deteriorating over time. The scorecard aggregates metrics from five dimensions, each weighted and combined into an overall health score that can be compared across teams and across sprints.

attrs.alt: Diagram: five scorecard dimensions — Eval Health, Security Posture, Operational, Process, and Cost Efficiency — with their constituent metrics and targets

content.text: Why a Scorecard Rather Than Individual Metrics

Individual metrics are easy to game: a team focused only on eval pass rate might neglect security posture; a team focused on latency might let process discipline slip. A scorecard with multiple dimensions forces holistic health tracking:

a team cannot score well on the overall scorecard by excelling in one dimension while failing in another. The weighted average means serious deficits in any dimension drag the total score below target.

the scorecard shows sprint-over-sprint trends. A team that scored 82 last sprint and 80 this sprint has a visible declining trend that warrants investigation, even though both scores are above the 70 red threshold.

when multiple Claude teams use the same scorecard schema, engineering leadership can compare teams without needing to understand the internals of each team's metrics. A score of 85 means the same thing regardless of which team or product generated it.

the scorecard is the primary artifact for the quarterly engineering review. Teams present their current score and the actions they are taking on red and amber metrics. This creates a clear accountability loop that individual metric dashboards do not.

A scorecard is not a performance rating of engineers. It is a measurement of the health of the system the team owns. A team that inherits a poorly instrumented, under-tested integration starts with a low score not because of poor performance but because of inherited technical debt. The scorecard's value is in tracking their trajectory.
Eval health is the primary signal for model behaviour quality. It measures whether the integration's behaviour is regressing, improving, or stable:
Flaky eval rate (cases with inconsistent result)
Golden dataset age (days since last review)
Each metric is scored 0–100, with healthy = 100, amber = 70, and red = 0. Intermediate values are interpolated linearly. The dimension score is the average of the five metric scores.

Security posture has the highest weight and the tightest targets of any dimension. A security failure in a Claude integration is not an operational inconvenience — it is a potential data breach, a trust violation, or an AI safety incident:

Security Posture is the only dimension where amber and red have the same consequence as red for scorecard classification: if any security metric is amber or red, the team's overall scorecard status is immediately classified as Red regardless of the weighted average. Security posture is binary — it either holds or it doesn't.
Operational health measures whether the integration is running reliably in production and whether the team is responding to operational problems quickly:
P95 latency as % of SLA (rolling 7 days)
Process discipline measures whether the team is following the engineering standards rather than bypassing them under delivery pressure:
Review SLA adherence (% of PRs reviewed within SLA)
DoD completion rate (% of features shipped with full DoD)
Gate bypass rate (% of releases with any gate bypassed)
Open release blockers (not resolved within 5 business days)
Cost per request as % of budget
Token budget compliance (% of templates with max_tokens set)
The overall scorecard score is the weighted average of the five dimension scores:
the scorecard script runs every Monday at 09:00, pulls metrics from CI results, GitHub, and monitoring dashboards, and posts the result to the team's #scorecard Slack channel.
the scorecard is reviewed at the end of each sprint. Any dimension below target requires the team to present a root-cause analysis and a remediation plan.

the last three sprints' scorecard trends are presented to engineering leadership. Teams with a sustained downward trend in any dimension are required to produce a formal remediation plan with a target date.
Team identifies at least one amber or red metric for remediation in the next sprint
Team presents root-cause analysis to EM; creates GitHub issues for each red metric with owner and target date
Security reviewer notified; team presents remediation plan regardless of overall score
Pulling metrics from existing data sources
The scorecard requires metrics from several systems. The data collection step runs as a GitHub Actions job that queries each source and writes a consolidated JSON file:
Several common mistakes undermine the value of the team health scorecard:
Treating the scorecard as a report card rather than a tool:

when teams focus on improving their scorecard score rather than the underlying system health, they optimise for the metric rather than the outcome. A team that adds eval cases without reviewing whether they test real failure modes will raise their coverage score without improving quality. Track the leading indicators (eval case quality, security review depth, runbook completeness) alongside the scorecard score.
Excluding new integrations from the scorecard:

some teams exclude newly launched Claude features from the scorecard on the grounds that they are 'still ramping up'. This creates a blind spot: new features are often the riskiest, and excluding them from the scorecard delays the feedback loop on their health.
Setting targets that are too tight for the current baseline:

if a team starts with an eval pass rate of 70% and the scorecard immediately flags them as Red, the scorecard creates pressure to cheat (delete failing cases) rather than to improve. Set realistic initial targets based on the current baseline and tighten them quarterly as the team improves.

a manually computed scorecard will be late, inaccurate, and inconsistently applied. All five dimensions must be computable from existing automated data sources. If a metric cannot be automated, replace it with a metric that can be.

a team scoring 82 three sprints in a row is in a different position from a team that scored 95, then 88, then 82. The declining trend is a leading indicator of a problem even if the absolute score is above the amber threshold. Track trend direction alongside the score.
The team health scorecard and per-release quality gates serve different purposes and should not be conflated:
Block bad releases before they ship
Track engineering health trends over time
Triggers team review and remediation planning

The gate is a safety net for individual releases. The scorecard is a health monitor for the team's system over time. A team can pass every release gate while the scorecard trends downward — for example, if gate pass rates are barely clearing the minimum, eval coverage is stagnant, and security issues are accumulating. The scorecard catches drift that the release gate misses.
Scorecard for New Teams vs Established Teams
Bootstrapping the scorecard on a new integration

When a team launches a new Claude integration, their scorecard will initially be low on dimensions that require data history (canary abort rate, rollback rate, incident rate). This is expected and should not trigger the same response as a declining score on an established integration. The bootstrapping approach:
In the first four weeks, mark history-dependent metrics as N/A rather than scoring them as zero.
After eight weeks of live operation, switch to normal scoring.
Set an initial baseline from the first eight weeks of data and tighten targets after the first quarter.
For the first quarter, focus scorecard reviews on the leading indicators (eval coverage, security posture, process discipline) rather than the lagging operational metrics.

Different Claude integrations have different risk profiles. A customer-facing invoice assistant has stricter security targets than an internal data extraction tool. The scorecard schema supports per-integration target overrides:

# scorecard-config.yml
defaults:
  eval_health_target: 85
  security_target: 100
  operational_target: 80
  process_target: 90
  cost_target: 75

overrides:
  invoice-assistant:
    security_target: 100        # same — no relaxation for customer-facing
    eval_health_target: 90      # stricter than default for customer-facing

  internal-data-extraction:
    security_target: 95         # slightly relaxed — internal use only, no PII
    eval_health_target: 80      # lower complexity task

The team health scorecard combines metrics from five dimensions — eval health, security posture, operational health, process discipline, and cost efficiency — into a single weekly score that tracks trends rather than point-in-time pass/fail. Security posture overrides the weighted average: any security deficit immediately classifies the scorecard as Red regardless of other scores. The scorecard creates accountability without requiring manual review of individual CI runs — teams and leadership can see at a glance whether the engineering health of a Claude integration is improving, stable, or deteriorating.
Eval Health and Security Posture each carry 30% of the weight, reflecting that behaviour quality and safety are the primary obligations of a Claude team.
Operational, Process, and Cost dimensions each carry 10–20%, ensuring they are tracked without dominating a team that is strong on core quality.
The scorecard is computed from existing data sources — CI results, GitHub metrics, monitoring dashboards — and does not require manual data entry.
Teams review scorecard trends in sprint reviews and quarterly engineering reviews; sustained decline in any dimension requires a formal remediation plan.
